---
fontsize: 9pt
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: pdflatex
    keep_tex: true
geometry: "a4paper, top=13mm, bottom=5mm, left=13mm, right=13mm"
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{xcolor}
  - \usepackage{enumitem}
  - \usepackage{parskip}
  - \usepackage{microtype}
  - \usepackage{helvet}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\sfdefault}
  - \pagenumbering{gobble}
  - \setlength{\parskip}{1.5pt}
  - \setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
  - \setlist{noitemsep, topsep=1pt, parsep=0pt, partopsep=0pt}
  - \definecolor{HohenheimNavy}{HTML}{004178}
  - \definecolor{HohenheimGreen}{HTML}{80CC00}
  - \definecolor{HohenheimDark}{HTML}{3C3C3B}
  - \definecolor{HohenheimAccent}{HTML}{AFC80A}
  - \definecolor{AccentRed}{HTML}{E63946}
  - \captionsetup{font=scriptsize, labelfont=bf, justification=raggedright, singlelinecheck=false, skip=1pt, hypcap=false}
  - \AtBeginDocument{\setlength{\parskip}{1.5pt}\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}}
  - \input{poster_vars.tex}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(readxl)

raw <- read_excel("WHR23_Data_Figure_2.1.xls")
df <- raw %>%
  select(country = `Country name`, score = `Ladder score`,
         gdp = `Logged GDP per capita`, social = `Social support`) %>%
  filter(!is.na(score) & !is.na(gdp) & !is.na(social))

n_countries <- nrow(df)
test_gdp    <- cor.test(df$gdp,    df$score)
test_social <- cor.test(df$social, df$score)
r_gdp       <- round(test_gdp$estimate,    2)
r_social    <- round(test_social$estimate, 2)
p_gdp       <- format.pval(test_gdp$p.value,    digits = 2, eps = 0.001)
p_social    <- format.pval(test_social$p.value,  digits = 2, eps = 0.001)
top1        <- df %>% slice_max(score, n = 1) %>% pull(country)

writeLines(c(
  "% Auto-generated by poster_new.Rmd",
  paste0("\\newcommand{\\nCountries}{", n_countries, "}"),
  paste0("\\newcommand{\\rGdp}{", r_gdp, "}"),
  paste0("\\newcommand{\\pGdp}{", p_gdp, "}"),
  paste0("\\newcommand{\\rSocial}{", r_social, "}"),
  paste0("\\newcommand{\\pSocial}{", p_social, "}"),
  paste0("\\newcommand{\\topOne}{", top1, "}"),
  "\\newcommand{\\postersection}[1]{%",
  "  \\vspace{3pt}%",
  "  {\\color{HohenheimGreen}\\bfseries\\normalsize #1}\\\\[-2.5mm]%",
  "  {\\color{HohenheimGreen}\\rule{\\linewidth}{1.2pt}}%",
  "  \\vspace{1pt}%",
  "}",
  "\\newcommand{\\postersubsec}[1]{%",
  "  \\smallskip{\\bfseries #1}\\par%",
  "}"
), "poster_vars.tex")

dir.create("figures", showWarnings = FALSE)

# Figure 1: GDP vs Score – points coloured by happiness (red=low, navy=high)
p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = gdp, y = score, color = score)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#E63946", high = "#004178", guide = "none") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#E63946",
              fill = "#E6394635", linewidth = 0.9) +
  annotate("label", x = 11.5, y = 2.5,
           label = paste0("r = ", r_gdp, "\np < 0.001"),
           hjust = 1, size = 2.1, color = "#004178", fontface = "bold",
           fill = "white", label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines")) +
  labs(x = "GDP per Capita (log)", y = "Happiness Score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 7) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.title  = element_text(size = 6.5, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        axis.text   = element_text(size = 6.0, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        plot.margin = margin(2, 4, 1, 2))
ggsave("figures/fig1.png", p1, width = 3.6, height = 1.6, dpi = 200)

# Bar chart: Average contribution of each WHR factor to happiness score
factor_names <- c("Explained by: Log GDP per capita",
                  "Explained by: Social support",
                  "Explained by: Healthy life expectancy",
                  "Explained by: Freedom to make life choices",
                  "Explained by: Generosity",
                  "Explained by: Perceptions of corruption")
factor_labels <- c("GDP per Capita", "Social Support",
                   "Life Expectancy", "Freedom",
                   "Generosity", "Low Corruption")
means <- sapply(factor_names, function(col) mean(raw[[col]], na.rm = TRUE))

factors_df <- data.frame(
  factor = factor_labels,
  contribution = as.numeric(means),
  highlight = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
) %>%
  arrange(contribution) %>%
  mutate(factor = factor(factor, levels = factor))

p2 <- ggplot(factors_df, aes(x = contribution, y = factor, fill = highlight)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(contribution, 2)),
            hjust = -0.15, size = 2.1, color = "#3C3C3B") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#004178", "FALSE" = "#A8C4DC")) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0.02, 0), limits = c(0, 1.65)) +
  labs(x = "Mean Contribution to Happiness Score", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 7) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
        axis.title.x = element_text(size = 6.5, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        axis.text    = element_text(size = 6.5, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        plot.margin  = margin(2, 8, 1, 2))
ggsave("figures/fig2.png", p2, width = 3.6, height = 1.5, dpi = 200)

# Figure 2 (poster): Social Support vs Score scatter, same style as fig1
p3 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = social, y = score, color = score)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#E63946", high = "#004178", guide = "none") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#E63946",
              fill = "#E6394635", linewidth = 0.9) +
  annotate("label", x = 0.98, y = 2.5,
           label = paste0("r = ", r_social, "\np < 0.001"),
           hjust = 1, size = 2.1, color = "#004178", fontface = "bold",
           fill = "white", label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines")) +
  labs(x = "Social Support", y = "Happiness Score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 7) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.title  = element_text(size = 6.5, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        axis.text   = element_text(size = 6.0, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        plot.margin = margin(2, 4, 1, 2))
ggsave("figures/fig3.png", p3, width = 3.6, height = 1.6, dpi = 200)

# Figure 4: Correlation (r) of each factor with happiness score
raw_factor_cols <- c("Logged GDP per capita", "Social support",
                     "Healthy life expectancy", "Freedom to make life choices",
                     "Generosity", "Perceptions of corruption")
cors <- sapply(raw_factor_cols, function(col) {
  x <- raw[[col]]; y <- raw[["Ladder score"]]
  ok <- !is.na(x) & !is.na(y)
  cor(x[ok], y[ok])
})

cors_df <- data.frame(
  factor = factor_labels,
  r = abs(as.numeric(cors)),   # abs() so "Low Corruption" reads positively like in Fig. 3
  highlight = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
) %>%
  arrange(r) %>%
  mutate(factor = factor(factor, levels = factor))

p4 <- ggplot(cors_df, aes(x = r, y = factor, fill = highlight)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", r)),
            hjust = -0.15, size = 2.1, color = "#3C3C3B") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#004178", "FALSE" = "#A8C4DC")) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0.02, 0), limits = c(0, 1.0)) +
  labs(x = "Correlation with Happiness Score (|r|)", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 7) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
        axis.title.x = element_text(size = 6.5, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        axis.text    = element_text(size = 6.5, color = "#3C3C3B"),
        plot.margin  = margin(2, 8, 1, 2))
ggsave("figures/fig4.png", p4, width = 3.6, height = 1.5, dpi = 200)
```

```{=latex}
\setlength{\parskip}{1.5pt}
\vbox to \textheight{%
\noindent\colorbox{HohenheimNavy}{%
  \parbox[c][26mm][c]{\textwidth}{%
    \hspace{4mm}%
    \begin{minipage}[c]{0.61\textwidth}
      {\color{white}\bfseries\Large What Makes People Happy?}\\[1.5mm]
      {\color{white}\normalsize An Analysis of the World Happiness Report 2023}
    \end{minipage}%
    \hfill
    \begin{minipage}[c]{0.32\textwidth}
      \raggedleft
      {\color{HohenheimAccent}\bfseries\footnotesize AUTHOR}\\[1mm]
      {\color{white}\footnotesize
      Mehmet Akif Coskun\\[0.3mm]
      Bachelor Business Informatics\\[0.3mm]
      University of Hohenheim}
    \end{minipage}%
    \hspace{4mm}
  }%
}\par\nopagebreak
{\color{HohenheimGreen}\rule{\textwidth}{5pt}}\par\nopagebreak
\vspace{3mm}\nopagebreak[4]
\noindent
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.44\textwidth}%
\vspace{0pt}%
\color{HohenheimDark}\footnotesize
\postersection{1.~~Introduction}

\begin{itemize}
  \item The \textbf{World Happiness Report (WHR)} has measured subjective well-being in 150+ countries since 2012. Respondents rate life satisfaction on the \textbf{Cantril ladder}: 0 = worst, 10 = best possible life.
  \item Key factors studied: economic output, social support, health, and freedom.
\end{itemize}

\postersection{2.~~Theoretical Background}

\postersubsec{Research Question}

Which socioeconomic factors correlate most strongly with subjective well-being?

\postersubsec{Hypotheses}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Countries with \textbf{higher GDP per capita} report higher happiness.
  \item \textbf{Stronger social support} is positively associated with happiness.
\end{enumerate}

\postersection{3.~~Method}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Dataset:} World Happiness Report 2023 (Helliwell et al., 2023)
  \item \textbf{Sample:} \nCountries{} countries (WHR 2023 uses 2020 to 2022 survey averages)
  \item \textbf{Instrument:} Gallup World Poll, Cantril ladder (0 to 10)
  \item \textbf{Analysis:} Pearson correlation ($r$) and significance test ($p$) in R
\end{itemize}

\postersection{4.~~Results}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{H1:} GDP per capita correlates strongly with happiness (\textbf{$r$ = \rGdp, $p$ \pGdp}; Fig.~1).
  \item \textbf{H2:} Social support is an even stronger predictor (\textbf{$r$ = \rSocial, $p$ \pSocial}; Fig.~2).
  \item Fig.~3: \textbf{GDP} and \textbf{Social Support} contribute most to the score. Bar size reflects the factor's numerical scale, not $r$.
  \item Fig.~4: Ranking of all 6 WHR factors by correlation $|r|$. \textbf{Social Support} and \textbf{GDP} lead; Generosity is essentially uncorrelated with happiness.
\end{itemize}

\postersection{5.~~Discussion \& Conclusion}

\begin{itemize}
  \item Both hypotheses confirmed. Social support outperforms GDP as a predictor by correlation ($r$ = \rSocial{} vs.\ $r$ = \rGdp; Fig.~4).
  \item \textit{Why is social support the better predictor?} A higher $r$ means points cluster more tightly around the trend line. Countries with high social support \emph{reliably} show high happiness, so we can predict a country's happiness more accurately from its social support than from its GDP.
  \item \textit{Why does GDP contribute more ``absolute points'' (Fig.~3)?} Fig.~3 shows how many score points each factor \emph{adds on average}, not how well it predicts. Logged GDP takes values from 7 to 11, social support only 0 to 1. The same variation therefore translates into more numeric points for GDP, purely because its scale is wider. This is a \textbf{scale effect}, not a stronger relationship. Correlation $r$ is scale free, so Fig.~4 is the fair comparison.
  \item \textit{Limitation:} Correlation $\neq$ causation; self-reports may be culturally biased.
  \item \underline{Conclusion:} \textbf{Economic security} and \textbf{social cohesion} are the key drivers of well-being.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{4mm}

{\color{HohenheimGreen}\rule{\linewidth}{1pt}}
\vspace{1.5pt}

{\scriptsize\color{HohenheimDark}
\textbf{References}\\
Helliwell, J.\,F. et al.\ (2023). \textit{World Happiness Report 2023.} Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN).\\
Gallup Inc.\ (2023). \textit{Gallup World Poll Methodology.} gallup.com
}

\end{minipage}%
\hspace{0.02\textwidth}%
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.54\textwidth}%
\vspace{0pt}%
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/fig1.png}
\captionof{figure}{\textbf{GDP \& Happiness (H1).} \nCountries{} countries; \textbf{$r$ = \rGdp}, \textbf{$p$ \pGdp}. Point colour: \textcolor{AccentRed}{red} = low happiness, \textcolor{HohenheimNavy}{navy} = high. Red line = OLS regression.}

\vspace{1mm}

\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/fig3.png}
\captionof{figure}{\textbf{Social Support \& Happiness (H2).} \nCountries{} countries; \textbf{$r$ = \rSocial}, \textbf{$p$ \pSocial}. Same style as Fig.~1. Points hug the regression line more tightly than in Fig.~1, which is what a higher correlation looks like.}

\vspace{1mm}

\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/fig2.png}
\captionof{figure}{\textbf{Factor Contributions.} Mean absolute contribution of all 6 WHR factors to the happiness score. \textcolor{HohenheimNavy}{\textbf{Navy}} = factors tested here. GDP leads due to its larger numerical scale, not stronger correlation ($r$).}

\vspace{1mm}

\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/fig4.png}
\captionof{figure}{\textbf{Factor Correlations.} Pearson $|r|$ of each factor with the happiness score. Unlike Fig.~3, this removes the scale effect: \textbf{Social Support} now leads GDP, confirming it is the stronger predictor.}

\end{minipage}
\vfill
}% end \vbox
```
